utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is significantly more effective than wo_skills across all five evaluation dimensions. The wi_skills report correctly clusters symptoms into a unified defrost-system failure hypothesis, explicitly distinguishes between sealed-system and replaceable-component failures, applies an age-weighted repair-vs-replace analysis with structured cost comparisons, provides clear pre-visit safety and equipment guidance, and breaks down cost transparency by parts and labor with contingency scenarios. utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality consulting reports that pass all six evaluation criteria. Both reports correctly apply cost-ratio analysis, reference the Frigidaire service bulletin, flag the circuit breaker safety issue, cite EPA 608 certification requirements, provide multi-appliance prioritization tied to the 10-day deadline, and identify the active compressor warranty. The wi_skills report is marginally more structured with explicit dollar thresholds per appliance and more detailed authorization language, while the wo_skills report leans slightly more toward replacement recommendations (which may be more conservative but equally defensible). However, the wi_skills agent explicitly stated the skill was 'not available' and did not invoke /appliance-repairer, meaning the skill's decision gates were not formally applied — both agents relied on the same input data and their own reasoning. Given that the skill was not actually invoked in the wi_skills run, the quality difference is negligible, and both outputs are functionally equivalent for the property manager's purposes. Slight edge to wi_skills for more granular authorization thresholds and cost caps, but both are strong outputs.

utility_details.judge_error: No critical runtime errors detected in either run. Both agents completed successfully and produced valid Markdown output files passing all three verifier checks. The wi_skills run invoked the /appliance-repairer skill as required and read additional reference files from the skill directory.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is substantially more effective than wo_skills across all five evaluation dimensions. The wi_skills agent leveraged the appliance-repairer skill framework to produce a structured report with: (1) an explicit sealed-system vs. replaceable-component triage (ruling out refrigerant recovery equipment), (2) a structured age-weighted repair-vs-replace recommendation with cost percentages and parts scarcity considerations, (3) a pre-visit safety gate table with explicit equipment preparation guidance, and (4) customer-facing cost transparency with parts/labor breakdown and contingency scenarios. The wo_skills agent, while producing a technically competent and well-organized report that correctly identified defrost system failure as the primary suspect and cross-linked symptoms, failed to deliver the key differentiating outputs: it did not explicitly state whether refrigerant recovery equipment is needed, did not provide a structured age-weighted recommendation beyond a brief advisory note, and included no customer cost transparency section with parts/labor breakdown. The practical impact is significant: a technician using the wi_skills report would arrive fully prepared with the right equipment, clear diagnostic focus, and customer expectation-setting, while the wo_skills report leaves critical preparation gaps.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is moderately more effective than wo_skills, primarily on J1. The wi_skills run invoked the appliance-repairer skill framework, which guided a more structured repair-vs-replace analysis including explicit cost ratio computation (56%) and a nuanced conclusion incorporating both the threshold and the service bulletin (recommending replacement rather than a blind repair). The wo_skills run, while surprisingly competent on most items (J2-J6 all pass), did not apply the 60% threshold gate in a visible, structured way for J1. Both runs correctly identified the EPA 608 requirement, warranty leverage, circuit-breaker safety flag, service bulletin implication, and multi-appliance prioritization. The skill provided clearest value in ensuring the repair-vs-replace threshold was applied rigorously and documented explicitly, which is the core differentiator in this scenario.

items.criterion: The report groups the reported symptoms (warm fresh food, cold freezer, clicking noise, ice maker failure, high electricity bill) into a coherent single-cause or two-cause hypothesis rather than listing them as unrelated issues. Pass: the report explicitly connects at least three symptoms to a shared probable cause (e.g., defrost system failure causing frost buildup blocking the evaporator fan). Fail: each symptom is addressed separately with no cross-linking.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report explicitly groups warm fresh food, cold freezer, clicking noise, ice maker failure, and high electricity bill into a shared defrost-system/airflow failure hypothesis, stating 'Classic side-by-side pattern' and cross-linking all symptoms under defrost-related causes.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report also connects the symptoms into a coherent hypothesis in its 'Initial Service Assessment' section, explicitly linking freezer still cold + warm fresh food + ice maker loss + higher power usage to a single airflow/defrost distribution failure narrative.

items.criterion: The report correctly identifies whether the most likely root cause involves the sealed system (compressor, refrigerant lines) or replaceable components (evaporator fan motor, defrost heater, defrost thermostat, defrost timer/control). Pass: the report explicitly states which category the suspected fault falls into and explains why this matters for the visit (e.g., whether refrigerant recovery equipment is needed). Fail: the report conflates sealed-system and component issues, or does not distinguish between them.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report lists 'Sealed system/compressor efficiency issue' as lower probability because the freezer is still cold, implicitly distinguishing sealed-system from component failures. However, it does not explicitly state that no refrigerant recovery equipment is needed — the distinction is present but not fully articulated for equipment preparation.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report explicitly states in Service Notes: 'prioritize airflow and defrost diagnosis before assuming a sealed-system leak,' directly distinguishing sealed-system from component faults. It similarly does not explicitly mention refrigerant recovery equipment but makes the category distinction clear.

items.criterion: The report provides a repair-vs-replace recommendation that explicitly references the appliance's age (11 years) as a factor, not just the parts cost. Pass: the recommendation addresses parts availability, likelihood of future failures on an 11-year-old unit, and compares estimated repair cost against replacement cost as a percentage. It mentions the appliance age and lists inspection priorities and parts, but provides no structured recommendation addressing parts availability, likelihood of future failures, or cost comparison against replacement.

items.criterion: The report tells the technician what safety checks to perform on arrival and what tools or equipment to bring based on the suspected fault category. Pass: the report specifies at minimum whether refrigerant recovery equipment is or is not required, and flags any safety pre-checks (electrical, water proximity). Fail: no equipment or safety preparation guidance is given.

items.criterion: The report includes a section or note intended to set cost expectations for the customer, distinguishing parts cost from labor cost and noting any scenario where the estimate could change after disassembly. Pass: parts and labor are addressed separately and at least one contingency is flagged. Fail: only a single total estimate is given with no breakdown or contingency.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced competent service reports that pass the basic verifier checks. For J1 (symptom clustering) and J2 (sealed vs. component distinction), both reports perform similarly well — they identify the defrost/airflow hypothesis and deprioritize sealed-system failure. Neither report satisfies J3 (age-weighted repair-vs-replace), J4 (explicit equipment/safety preparation guidance including refrigerant recovery statement), or J5 (cost transparency with parts/labor breakdown and contingency). The wi_skills report benefits from the skill workflow invocation and produces a slightly more structured narrative with clearer symptom grouping labels, but it does not demonstrate the key differentiators described in the expected outcome — no explicit 'no refrigerant recovery equipment needed' statement, no age-weighted repair-vs-replace gate, and no cost expectations section. The wo_skills report is marginally more explicit about the sealed-system exclusion reasoning in its Service Notes. Overall, the two outputs are nearly equivalent in quality, with neither achieving the full expected outcome. wi_skills has a slight edge in report organization and symptom labeling, but the substantive differentiating criteria (J3, J4, J5) are unmet by both.

items.criterion: The write-up documents that the simple/cheap drain causes (clogged filter, kinked hose) were checked first, records what was found, and explains why the fault was not resolved after the filter was cleared — justifying the escalation to the drain pump motor as the next hypothesis. Pass: the write-up presents a logical progression from simple to complex with findings at each step. Fail: the write-up jumps directly to motor/control board without documenting the simpler checks or treating the filter clearing as a resolved issue.

Pass: the write-up names the part(s) to order, estimates the return visit scope, and gives the dispatch office enough information to schedule correctly. utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher-quality write-up than wo_skills. Both pass J1, J3, and J4. However, wi_skills additionally passes J2 (safety gate: explicitly clears the burning smell as motor-related with no scorch marks found, advises unplug if smell returns) and J5 (implicitly rules out electrician referral with rationale tied to on-site findings), while wo_skills fails both. The wi_skills output is more structured, includes a dedicated Safety/Customer Communication section, a dedicated Warranty/Coverage section with clear billable labor guidance, and a numbered Return Visit Focus list — all elements that a dispatch office needs. Both runs fail J6 (no concrete parts lead time or second-visit date). Overall, wi_skills is clearly more effective for the stated purpose.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully with all verifier checks passing.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality consulting reports that addressed most evaluation criteria. wi_skills scores 6/6 while wo_skills scores 5/6. The key differentiator is J6 (warranty leverage): the wi_skills report explicitly advises checking warranty eligibility before authorizing any parts expenditure, while wo_skills mentions the warranty but dismisses it as unlikely to reduce cost — missing the requirement to pursue the warranty claim as a mandatory first step. In all other dimensions (threshold calculation, service bulletin integration, safety gate for the washer, EPA 608 requirement, and multi-appliance prioritization), both reports are comparably strong. The wi_skills report is slightly more structured and actionable overall, reflecting the skill's decision framework, but the wo_skills report is surprisingly capable and only falls short on the warranty leverage guidance.
